I find the idea that many members of the GOP party use their political platform to publicly speak against abortion, and certain forms of birth control such as Plan B are due to private adoption agencies experiencing shortages of adoptable children to be somewhat credible. The content present in the first draft opinion of the Supreme Court in the case of Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization, as well as the majority of religious right-wing individuals not holding a negative view on the concept of abortion in the United States until well into the 1970's (well after the Supreme Court Ruling on Roe v. Wade). According to the CDC, of the nearly 4,000,000 American children who are born each year, only about 18,000 are voluntarily relinquished for adoption. Though the statistics vary, some estimates suggest that dozens of couples are now waiting to adopt each available baby. The decline in the numbers of adoptable children began in the mid 1970's, coinciding with the beginning of the GOP Party and the religious right's stance against abortion, which had been previouslt mostly nonexistent. The percentage of single mothers who relinquish their infants has declined from nearly 9% to less than 1%. However, after the Supreme Court's ruling overturning Roe v. Wade, many states that have right-wing governments in power have enacted abortion restrictions. With many women no longer having easy access to abortion, the percentage of children voluntarily relinquished for adoption will now grow. ||| I would be interested in exploring additional factors and perspectives. I agree that this topic requires nuanced thought, however I currently stand by my opinion that increasing the amount of adoptable children was a factor in many of the decisions regarding abortion and birth control restrictions. I do want to point out that I don't believe that is the sole factor, though. I believe it is just one of a plethora of factors and opinions. ||| I definitely agree that this is a multifacted issue, and some great points were made. However, I do disagree with some key stances outlined in the above statements. Societal shifts are trending towards smaller families, and couples and individuals are choosing to be child-free at much higher rates than in previous decades. If this trend were to be incorporated into the broader societal structure, then logic dictates that access to abortion and birth control should increase, not decrease. 

In the above statement, it was also posited that the GOP and religious right tends fall into a pro-life ethic, where life is protected at all stages. However, if the GOP and religious right aligned themselved with protecting life, then it would stand to reason that the common platform wouldn't be in support of the death penalty. Support of the death penalty constitutes a direct contradition to the sanctity of life. ||| Unfortunately I don't see any immediate reconciliation of these different views and positions without major changes in the overall approach to abortion, birth control, and family planning/healthcare in general. I feel there are several factors in these trends, but I still feel that increasing the amount of adoptable children for private adoption agencies was still a potential factor.

Back in the 1980's Coca-Cola introduced "New Coke."  It turned into a controversy and everyone was talking about whether or not they liked it.  Many people hated it and demanded that they bring back regular Coca-Cola, which they eventually did.  The theory is that Coca-Cola released an inferior product on purpose.  Everyone wanted to try New Coke and compare it to the original Coca-Cola, so sales increased significantly.  Then sales increased again when they brought back the original, because people wanted to taste it again.  The company denied it, but this theory is compelling because it seems completely plausible.  What large corporation doesn't want to increase sales? I don't have any specific evidence, but I was alive then and I distinctly remember this craze.  Everyone I knew was talking about New Coke and debating about which drink was better.  We were getting together at school and trying it.  I remember it being on TV.  The general consensus among people I knew was that it was nowhere near as good as original Coca-Cola.  It seems like Coca-Cola would have run some focus groups and tested it out before releasing the product if this really was a serious "improvement." ||| Can you tell me what the financial impact of New Coke was to Coca-Cola?  How much money did they lose?  Attention and publicity also has value.  As you mentioned, Coke was losing to Pepsi, which was seen as "cooler" and better tasting.  New Coke put all of the attention back onto Coca-Cola, and then they were seen as doing the right thing by bringing back the original product, which makes them look trustworthy and like they care about their customers.  We drank lots of Coca-Cola Classic after this.  Even if the whole incident cost them money, they still won in the end.  Pepsi turned into a fad and Coca-Cola is iconic. ||| What if it's not a misstep?  Coke was losing the battle against Pepsi.  But there is clearly a strong emotional connection to Coca-Cola that simply isn't there with Pepsi.  What if Coca-Cola realized that this emotional connection existed, and that it was the one thing that separated them from Pepsi?

Pepsi was definitely a fad in the 80's, thanks to Max Headroom and the Coke vs. Pepsi commercials.  Now PepsiCo is hugely successful as a brand, but Pepsi the soda is not as popular as Coca-Cola.  I couldn't even tell you the last time I saw someone drink a Pepsi.  We are not emotionally attached to the flavor of Pepsi. ||| You may be right, and we'll never know.  PepsiCo is a huge brand now, you're correct, but they have been forced to diversify their product base significantly.  But when only comparing their namesake sodas, you'll find that Coca-Cola is much more popular.  The New Coke incident didn't hurt them in the long run -- it actually boosted brand loyalty, bonded their customers, and created a moment in pop culture that we are still talking about nearly 40 years later.

I think that conspiracy theories about 9/11, while they often miss the mark in terms of what I think is plausible and realistic, do sometimes mirror my own perceptions of what motivates the United States' foreign policy and other decisions. I don't think it's reasonable to imagine some cabal of lizard people pulling the strings or anything like that, but some 9/11 conspiracy theories portray the Bush administration as having enough motivation to enable such an attack. I think that theories that point out members of the Bush Administration's financial gains from the Iraq war, the ways in which the attacks benefitted former president Bush's domestic agendas and the extent to which former president Bush and other members of his administration were willing to lie in the years that followed the attacks to justify military action in the middle east all lend credibility to the tamer varieties of 9/11 conspiracy theories. I also think these theories have a pretty strong emotional pull; the humanitarian unrest in other parts of the world as a result of our government's actions make it easy to imagine the Bush Administration as having orchestrated or allowed for attacks on its own citizens. ||| I agree that it requires a lot of assumptions to claim that the Bush administration orchestrated the 9/11 attacks, even given the lies and questionable motivations of the administration and its members, and that just because some of them stood to gain financially from the conflicts that followed we can't assume that they were willing to take political and military action solely for their own benefit. However, I think that there is more to it than simply the motivations of the Bush Administration and its members. I think that before George W. Bush ever took office, the United States' government and economy were deeply engrossed in the nation's global military interests. Unreasonable though it may be to assert that the Bush Administration is solely responsible for the 9/11 attacks, the socioeconomic context of the United States and the extent to which the nation's economy was and still is centered around the military-industrial complex do, in my opinion, make theories about the Bush Administration's involvement in or knowledge of the attacks more plausible. While there are elements of the theory that may not hold up to scrutiny, I don't know that we have all the information, and I generally have found that the government of the United States, under various presidential administrations, is willing to engage in dubiously ethical military practice for the sake of economic gain. ||| I agree that there's a lot of nuance, it's why I'm apprehensive about various aspects of 9/11 conspiracies. That's why I was sure to mention the emotional pull of the theories initially; I recognize in myself that, even though theories about 9/11 are likely based on false assumptions, I'm willing to lend them more credence than I otherwise might based on my preexisting opinions about the United States' government. On the other hand, healthy skepticism is the process of taking this potentially circumstantial evidence and fleshing it out with additional definitions and research. I do believe that the line between these two can be very thin at times, healthy skepticism can lead to a conspiracy theory, and a conspiracy theory can be realized with well-intentioned skepticism.

This is very niche but the one conspiracy theory I think might be true is about the sugar lobby in the 1960s, who were angry at President Kennedy for the Cuban embargo, which affected their businesses. Companies like PepsiCo complained publicly about Kennedy and frequently lobbied to try to change the embargo. On the same weekend that Kennedy went to Dallas in November 1963, there was a national soda bottlers' convention being held as well. Pepsi had hired Richard Nixon, who had lost to Kennedy in the presidential run a few years earlier, to appear at the convention, and Nixon slammed Kennedy in the press the day before he was assassinated, not to mention started a false rumor that Kennedy was going to drop Johnson as his VP. Pepsi (not the only company to do this, of course) was trying to turn their business problems into political issues to force Kennedy to change his stance on the embargo. Nixon was reportedly nervous the entire time he was in Dallas, didn't even attend the convention (which was the supposed reason he was there), and when JFK was shot, was visibly shaken and immediately took to telling multiple people that he didn't have anything to do with it, long before anyone even thought that maybe he did. Given Nixon's later manipulation of peace talks between Johnson and North Vietnam, I think it's possible Nixon was either involved or knew a lot more about the assassination than he ever let on. There have been a few articles over the years, some of them focusing more on Joan Crawford who was also there in her capacity as an executive officer of Pepsi at the time. This also comes up in Dorothy Killgallen biographies and conspiracies, it's mentioned in Nixon biographies, and of course the more fringe JFK conspiracy corners of the internet. Mainly I'm interested in the personal observations of those who were around Nixon those two days in Dallas. ||| What are the details of the sugar lobby opposition to Kennedy's Cuban embargo? ||| How many acres of sugar plantations did PepsiCo own in 1963? ||| Pepsi owned enough sugar plantations in Cuba that they were able to sell land there worth $6.3 million in 1950. These were all taken over by the government in 1962.

These theories typically suggest secretive, malevolent forces working behind the scenes. It's important to note that while some conspiracy theories may be based on legitimate concerns, many lack credible evidence and can contribute to misinformation.

 one example of a conspiracy theory is the "moon landing hoax" theory, which suggests that the United States faked the Apollo moon landings in the 1960s. Proponents of this theory claim that the moon landings were staged on Earth for various reasons, such as winning the space race against the Soviet Union or distracting the public from other issues.

However, it's important to note that this conspiracy theory has been thoroughly debunked. The Apollo moon landings were extensively documented, and there is overwhelming evidence supporting their authenticity, including photographs, videos, rock samples, and the accounts of thousands of people involved in the missions.

Conspiracy theories often arise due to skepticism, misinformation, or a distrust of official narratives. Debunking such theories involves presenting verifiable evidence and relying on the scientific method to establish the truth. ||| Absolutely, you've provided a comprehensive set of evidence that supports the authenticity of the Apollo moon landings.
